title: Somatic Therapy for Grief: When Loss Lives in the Body
excerpt: Explore how somatic therapy helps release grief stored in the body, offering relief beyond talk therapy.
body:
Understanding Grief Beyond the Mind

Grief isn’t just a mental or emotional experience—it’s deeply embodied. When we lose someone or something significant, the pain often gets stuck not only in our hearts but in our bodies. Traditional talk therapy can help process the thoughts and feelings around loss, but many people find themselves still feeling physically tense, numb, or overwhelmed long after the sessions end. This is where somatic therapy can make a profound difference.

Somatic therapy focuses on the body’s role in emotional experience and healing. It works with the nervous system, bodily sensations, and implicit memory to address how trauma and grief are stored physically. As Dr. Bessel van der Kolk explains in The Body Keeps the Score, unresolved trauma and stress manifest as chronic tension, pain, and dysregulation in the body. Grief, often traumatic in its own right, follows a similar pattern.

Why Grief Lives in the Body

When you experience loss, your nervous system shifts into states designed for survival: fight, flight, freeze, or collapse. Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory highlights how the autonomic nervous system reacts to threat, including emotional threats like grief, by altering heart rate, breathing, and muscle tone. These shifts can linger, creating a sensation of being “stuck” in grief.

Somatic researcher and therapist Peter Levine points out that grief can trigger a freeze response, where the body immobilizes to protect itself from overwhelming emotions. This immobilization leads to sensations of heaviness, numbness, or dissociation. Over time, the body enacts holding patterns—chronic muscle tension, restricted breathing, and postural changes—that keep the grief alive in physical form.

Common Physical Signs of Grief

  • Chest tightness or difficulty breathing
  • Fatigue and low energy
  • Muscle tension, especially in the neck, shoulders, and jaw
  • Digestive disturbances
  • Headaches or migraines
  • Sleep disturbances
  • Numbness or disconnection from the body

These symptoms often resist resolution through talk therapy alone because they are embedded in the body’s physiology.

Somatic Therapy: A Mind-Body Approach to Grief

Somatic therapy integrates body awareness and movement with psychological insight. It helps individuals access sensations and impulses that are outside of conscious thought but directly related to unresolved grief. This approach aligns with Dr. Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, which emphasizes tracking bodily sensations as a gateway to processing trauma and grief.

Unlike traditional therapy that focuses on verbalizing emotions, somatic therapy trains clients to notice how grief feels physically and to develop skills in nervous system regulation. This includes grounding techniques, breath work, and gentle movement to release tension and restore a sense of safety.

“Healing grief means learning to feel the body’s messages and letting the nervous system complete what it was forced to stop.” – Bessel van der Kolk

Key Components of Somatic Therapy for Grief

  • Body Awareness: Cultivating attention to physical sensations related to grief.
  • Nervous System Regulation: Techniques to calm hyperarousal or activate the body out of freeze states.
  • Movement and Expression: Using gentle movement to discharge stuck energy and re-engage vitality.
  • Resource Building: Developing internal and external supports to contain difficult emotions.
  • Integration: Connecting bodily experience with emotions and memories to process loss fully.

Scientific Support for Somatic Therapy in Grief Recovery

Research shows that somatic approaches can effectively reduce symptoms of complicated grief and trauma. For example, Francine Shapiro’s Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR), which integrates body sensations with cognitive processing, has been shown to facilitate grief healing by accessing nonverbal trauma stored in the nervous system.

Dan Siegel’s work on interpersonal neurobiology also supports somatic therapy, emphasizing how the integration of body and mind improves emotional regulation and resilience. Deb Dana’s application of Polyvagal Theory provides practical tools for calming the nervous system during grief, helping individuals move from survival to restoration.

Practical Somatic Tools for Self-Directed Grief Healing

If you’re feeling stuck in grief despite therapy, you can begin somatic healing practices on your own. Here are some evidence-based techniques:

1. Body Scan Meditation

Lie down or sit comfortably and slowly bring your attention to different parts of your body. Notice areas of tension or numbness without judgment. This practice increases body awareness and helps identify where grief is held.

2. Grounding Exercises

Feel your feet on the floor, notice the support beneath you, and take slow, deep breaths. Grounding connects you to the present moment and calms the nervous system, as Deb Dana advises.

3. Breath Regulation

Practice slow diaphragmatic breathing to reduce hyperarousal. Inhale deeply for a count of four, hold for four, then exhale for six. This pattern activates the parasympathetic nervous system, promoting relaxation.

4. Gentle Movement

Engage in slow stretches, yoga, or shaking out your limbs to discharge stuck energy. Peter Levine emphasizes movement as a way to complete the body’s natural trauma response.

5. Expressive Touch

Placing a hand on your heart or belly can provide soothing sensory input. This practice helps regulate the nervous system and fosters self-compassion.

When to Seek a Somatic Therapist

While self-directed techniques are helpful, working with a trained somatic therapist can accelerate healing, especially if grief is complicated by trauma or chronic dysregulation. Therapists trained in Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, Somatic Experiencing, or EMDR can guide you through safely accessing and releasing grief held in the body.

Look for professionals who understand the nervous system and have experience with grief and trauma. They can help you identify your unique somatic patterns and tailor interventions that fit your needs.

The Journey of Grief Is Nonlinear

Remember that grief is not a linear process. It ebbs and flows, sometimes returning with a visceral intensity that can take you by surprise. Somatic therapy equips you with tools to meet these waves with awareness and compassion instead of resistance or avoidance.

As Gabor Maté reminds us, healing involves “listening to the body’s wisdom”. When loss lives in the body, honoring that wisdom is the pathway to transformation.

Conclusion

Grief is a deeply embodied experience that often requires more than verbal processing to heal. Somatic therapy addresses the physical imprint of loss by working directly with the nervous system and bodily sensations. Supported by research and clinical practice, somatic approaches offer a powerful path to release, regulation, and renewed connection with life.

If you’ve tried traditional therapy but still feel stuck in grief, consider integrating somatic healing practices or working with a somatic therapist. Your body holds the key to unlocking the grief that words alone cannot reach.

References

  • van der Kolk, B. (2015). The Body Keeps the Score. Penguin Books.
  • Levine, P. A. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. W. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy, Third Edition: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures. Guilford Press.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Maté, G. (2018). When the Body Says No: The Cost of Hidden Stress. Wiley.
  • Siegel, D. J. (2012). The Developing Mind: How Relationships and the Brain Interact to Shape Who We Are. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy differ from traditional talk therapy for grief?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on bodily sensations and nervous system regulation, addressing grief stored physically, while traditional talk therapy centers on verbalizing thoughts and emotions.

faqs.q: Can I practice somatic grief healing on my own?
faqs.a: Yes, techniques like body scans, grounding, breathwork, and gentle movement can be practiced independently to help release grief held in the body.

faqs.q: When should I consider seeing a somatic therapist for grief?
faqs.a: If grief feels overwhelming, persistent, or complicated by trauma, working with a trained somatic therapist can provide tailored support and safety.

faqs.q: Is somatic therapy supported by scientific research?
faqs.a: Yes, somatic therapy methods like Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, EMDR, and Polyvagal-informed approaches have empirical support for treating grief and trauma.
